behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive errors.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error construction in local code. No OS/network/subprocess dependency. A focused in-process parser test can catch real failures, including wrong field-range checks and poor error text.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with a table of invalid cron strings (`13` month, `32` day, truncated fields, bad tokens). Assert it returns/throws a structured error with field-specific message content (e.g., mentions invalid month/day and expected range/syntax).

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including `L` (last day of month) and `W` (nearest weekday).
behaviors.justification: Real risk is interaction between parser semantics (`L`/`W`) and run-time expansion logic across month boundaries. Pure unit tests on one helper can miss cross-module semantic mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Using real parser + schedule calculator modules, feed cron expressions with `L` and `W` from fixed start instants. Assert next N timestamps match authoritative expected sequences (including Feb in leap/non-leap years and weekend-adjusted weekdays).

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules that fire within configured minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: Contention detection depends on combined behavior of schedule generation + gap/window comparison + reporting format. Mocking either side can hide boundary bugs (exactly-at-gap, near-midnight, cross-day windows).
behaviors.test_description: Create two real cron schedules through parser/calculator, generate occurrences over a horizon, run overlap detector with a 5-minute gap, and assert returned conflict windows include expected start/end pairs and exclude non-conflicts at exact threshold boundaries.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling is correct across DST spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real timezone/DST rule behavior from runtime/OS tzdata. Mock timezone logic can falsely pass and miss production failures caused by real zone database transitions and ambiguous/nonexistent local times.
behaviors.test_description: Run engine in an environment with real tzdata (e.g., `America/New_York`). For known DST dates, assert spring-forward handling for nonexistent `02:30` (documented policy: skip/shift) and fall-back handling for duplicated `02:30` (single vs double fire per policy), verifying actual produced UTC/local timestamps.

behaviors.description: If computed next run is already in the past after downtime, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill).
behaviors.justification: Behavior spans schedule computation, persisted last-run state, and policy decision logic. A pure function test may miss real failures in state wiring and execution planning.
behaviors.test_description: With real scheduler components and a local persistence layer (or in-memory repo used by production code paths), simulate downtime by advancing clock past one or more expected fires. Assert `skip` policy schedules only the next future run, while `backfill` policy emits the expected catch-up run set and updates state correctly.
